params.text:
The environment and the small things

Person-centred care lives in the details. The environment and small everyday things have a huge impact on how comfortable, safe and valued a person feels.

  • Comfort — check lighting, temperature, noise and unpleasant odours, and reposition or support people who are in pain or discomfort.
  • Food and drink — honour preferences, cultural and religious requirements, and the simple pleasure of a favourite meal or hot drink.
  • Routines — respect the rhythms that matter to a person, such as when they like to wake, bathe or have quiet time.
  • Familiar things — photographs, music and personal belongings help maintain identity and a sense of home.

Noticing and acting on these small things is often what turns a task into truly person-centred care.

Putting it all together

Working in a person-centred way means keeping the individual at the centre of everything: knowing their story, following and updating their care plan, gaining consent, enabling active participation, supporting choice and control, protecting dignity, meeting holistic needs, and getting the small things right.

Do this consistently and you meet the expectations of Care Certificate Standard 5, CQC Regulation 9 and the values that sit at the heart of good adult social care.
